sentences.text: Yes.
I-- like I said, it was the first time in my life a free man, and fighting for my own country.
Even if I had to die, I would die for my country.
And I was all excited about the life, and everything.
Even though, until I got to Bayt Jibrin I didn't know what Israel meant because I didn't see a city.
We didn't stop anywhere except in Bayt Lid.
We didn't see Haifa.
We didn't see Tel Aviv or anything.
But when-- in Bayt Jibrin, we stayed for a month, then our unit was transferred to Hatsor, which is near Rehovot.
And at that time, the Palmach was being disbanded, and joining together the-- Tzvah Haganah L'Israel, the Israeli army, and the Palmach was made into one unit.
We stayed a few weeks just waiting for this.
And since I found out that we're going to be with the Tzvah Haganah, I at the same time made a friend that was-- we had some vacation.
We came from the front, from Bayt Jibrin.
I got a week's vacation, a hotel in Tel Aviv, and some good food, and everything else.
I met a friend.
His name was Asha Kruger.
He was in the Kheil HaAvir, in the Israeli Air Force.
And he said, look, you are finished mechanic.
They need badly mechanics in the Air Force.
And I suggest you apply.
I went and I applied.
And they took me into the Kheil HaAvir.
I was stationed in Tel Nof, which the main airbase was, near Rehovot.
And there I-- myself with other guys and our unit were trained for aircraft mechanics.
We started immediately working with some Italian mechanics which were, were actually hired by the Israeli government to work on the overhaul of motors for the B-17 and the spitfires.
I was trained to work on the motors for the B-17, which was a radial engine made by Pratt and Whitney.
And I was studying on my own.
And with the help of the Italian mechanics, in no time, many of us became qualified, good mechanics.
And the Italians were later on let go, and we took over.
Most of the Israeli guys took over.
I was very happy to be in the Air Force.
We were going to the city, visiting.
I made some friends.
And the only thing that many of the guys had families-- sisters, brothers, or somebody, to go to for Shabbat.
And I didn't have.
But this friend of mine used to take me to his parents'.
His parents-- Asha Kruger, his parents were just arrived from Poland.
And they were in a [NON-ENGLISH] Israel refugee camp, or immigrant camp.
And they were quite nice to me.
And I met many other people that were very nice to me and used to invite me for Shabbat.
When I finished the Kheil HaAvir, there were many jobs available for mechanics.
And I applied to El Al.
El Al hired me.
And I started to work as an aircraft mechanic in El Al.
Sent to some courses to bring up to date with the civil aviation, and DC-4 aircraft and constellation.
At that time, they just purchased some constellation airplanes.
And we were studying those airplanes.
And all the time I haven't had any family except, while I was in the Air Force, I forgot to mention, I heard a name mentioned, Waks.
And I came over to the guy, and I said, your name is Waks?
He says, yes.
I say, what's your first name?
He said, Shabtai.
And I found that he was my first cousin, from a city next to Stepan called Sarny.
He and his sister survived the Holocaust from the ghetto in Sarny.
His name was Shabtai Waks.
And a sister was Rosa Shtainberg.
She was already married.
And finally I had a little bit of family in Israel.
But not long after, I found out that I had a first cousin that also survived in Russia and came to Germany, to Bavaria.
And we started corresponding.
He was inviting me to come over and visit him and so on.
When the time came, and I got a chance to fly to-- after a year working for El Al, to fly to Germany, at that time Israel didn't have any relations with Germany.
So I went to Vienna.
I got a pass to go to Vienna.
And from Vienna I-- not knowing that how to get to Germany without any papers, I got to near Salzburg border.
And I was used to the partisans.
So I came to the forest, and I crossed the border illegally and came into Germany to visit my cousin.
I stayed with my cousin in Germany for a few weeks.
And then I wanted to go back to Israel.
He said, look, he says, I'm making papers.
I have an aunt in Milwaukee.
You've gone through so much, and I suggest that you go with us.
I spoke to my aunt.
I wrote to my aunt, and she said she'd be glad to sponsor you.
You can go with me.
And in Milwaukee you could get a job.
I said, no, I don't think so.
I'll go back.
In the meantime, I, I got some vacation, more vacation.
I wrote to El Al that I would like to stay here longer.
I was-- I met a guy by the name of [?
Norman ?] [?
Lachance ?] who was in the American Air Force.
And we started to talk, and he said they need mechanics in the Air Force.
I went and applied, and I got a job in the Air Force in the town of Erding.
It was a big airbase in Bavaria in Erding.
And I worked-- I lived in a youth hostel and worked for the American Air Force as a mechanic.
They send me on a course to finish hydraulic mechanic.
And I worked as a hydraulic mechanic on landing gears for the new jets, which were the F-86 at the time.
And since I worked there, I just decided to stay for a little while.
I was-- let's face it, in Israel times were very tough.
Here the food was good, and everything else.
And my cousin was assisting-- Shabtai Waks was his name.
That's my uncle's son from Stepan.
He's the whole time trying to talk me into to stay and go to the States.
He was still-- he didn't leave yet until for while.
Then the Korean War broke out.
And somehow, I felt that they had enough of wars, and I didn't want to go to the States or anywhere else.
My friend Norman Lachance, who was Canadian, said, well, if you're not going to the States, why don't you come to Canada?
I didn't know much about Canada except when I was in Israel, and I was fantasizing about some countries, sometimes where there's gold.
And I remember looking at a map and reading about Yellow Knife in Ontario, in Ontario where there were gold mines.
But otherwise, I had never heard about Canada except that there were some aircraft manufacturers that were made in Montreal by Canadair.
Well, Norman Lachance convinced me to apply to the Canadian embassy.
And I went, and I applied.
And within a matter of-- I don't know, must have been two years later or so, I, I worked there in the Air Force.
And I got the papers from the Canadian government to say that they have a job for me in Winnipeg as an aircraft mechanic to work for the Canadian Air Force.
Well, I decided I don't want to stay in Germany.
I never wanted to live or stay in Germany.
Was just the fact that I wanted to see my cousin to visit.
And I had to stay for three years in Germany because the requirement, in order to go for Canada, you had to be a domicile, or live in a country for three years.
Otherwise, they wouldn't let you in.
So I figure, I have a job, and I was a earning fairly well in the American Air Force.
So I stayed there three years.
When three years were up, the Canadian government issued a visa in Frankfurt.
And in September 1955 I left Bremenhaven to Halifax, arrived on the 27th of September to Halifax.
And from Halifax, I went to on the train to Montreal.
While I was on the boat, I was told that Winnipeg was very cold and a small city and there's not much of aircraft industries.
So I decided get off in Montreal.
And I had saved some money.
I think I had, already-- I was a rich man-- $2,000 saved with me I kept bringing from my worked in Germany.
And I was independent man.
When immigration came over in Montreal, to meet me, I said that I'm not going to Winnipeg.
They had a ticket for me, to Winnipeg.
I said, I'm going to stay in Montreal.
They were quite upset.
They said, you're on your own.
I said that's fine.
I took a taxi.
Fortunately, it was a Jewish man in the taxi.
And I asked him if he knows somebody I can rent a room.
And he took me over to some Hungarian religious family, and I rented a room from them on Hutchison Street in Montreal.
And the next day I went to the employment to look for a job.
I-- I went to the employment, and they said they didn't have any jobs in Montreal, but they had some in Saint-Jeans, Quebec.
It was a requirement for an aircraft mechanic.
Before I went to apply for a job Saint-Jeans, Quebec, I had a close friend, Michael [NON-ENGLISH] that lived in Toronto.
So he invited me over.
I came by train to Toronto.
I visited him, and I also went to apply for a job at Air Canada here in Toronto.
And they didn't have any openings.
So I went back to Montreal, and went to the job that I was offered in Saint-Jeans, Quebec.
I moved to Saint-Jeans, Quebec.
The Immigration Department-- Canadian immigration-- were very nice to me.
They right away came to visit me to see if I'm being treated well.
I lived in Richelieu Hotel there.
And I worked for the aircraft industry, so I had to pass an exam.
I remember Chief Inspector Jackson, Mr.
Jackson, said that I was qualified to be an inspector, not a mechanic.
So I became an inspector for aircraft industries of Canada.
I worked there for about half a year.
I found it very lonesome.
And there were no Jewish families living in Saint-Jeans, Quebec.
I didn't have any friends.
So I used to go to Montreal.
And I met a few-- made a few friends, already, in Montreal.
And within six months I had a call from Aviation Electric, where I applied.
It was a corporation that belonged to Bendix Corporation of Canada.
And they-- there I worked for about six months as a mechanic in hydraulics.
And then Air Canada called me that they have an opening.
And I went to work for Air Canada online, which means outside on the airplanes, servicing the airplanes.
And I worked until [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1957 with Air Canada.
I had some friends that were friends of my close friend George Kokh that was-- moved from the States-- from Paris to the States.
And I was in touch with him.
He came to visit me.
And I met his friends in Montreal.
His friend said, if you're looking for a room-- I was living in a room somewhere else.
He says, I have got some good cousins that have a nice room for you.
And I suggest that you go there.
You will like it.
I went and I met Mr.
And Mrs.
Conner.
And I lived in their room.
They were very nice to me.
And Mr. and Mrs.
Conner had close friends, which were Mr. and Mrs.
Diamond-- my present in-laws-- and they introduced me to my wife, Sonia, which was, all of a sudden, I was all excited to meet a pretty young lady.
And she was, at that time, in high school only.
And we started to go out.
She was just finishing high school.
And I was bugging her to go out more often, but she was too busy doing her matrics.
When she finished her matrics, we started to go out more seriously.
And then just about maybe a year after we knew each other, I proposed the marriage when I came.
And she accepted the proposal, and we got engaged.
That's in 1957-- beginning of 1957.
And we got married June the 23rd, 1957.
It was actually my happiest day of my life because, because I've never really had a family.
I was dreaming to get settled once and for all and maybe start a family and have some kind of a normal life.
And my wife's name is Sonia.
And she-- her parents were very nice to me, very, very nice to me-- Mr. and Mrs.
Diamond.
We have, now, thank God, three children-- three daughters.
Anna.
She was born February the 28th, 1959.
It was a real, again, excitement for us, especially for me being left all alone and just a brother after such a Holocaust and what we went through.
And then we were blessed with another daughter, born February the 14th, 1960.
Her name is Yona.
And then we were blessed with another beautiful daughter, the youngest, Sheila, born November 16th, 1964-- all in Montreal.
I was in touch with my brother while I was in Poland.
But, for a while, the Soviet authorities wouldn't let any mail to go.
And then from Israel, somehow I got in touch with him.
And I found out in Israel that-- really, I didn't know about my second brother, Yakov, that was killed in 1947 after the war.
Nobody told me about it.
I didn't know until I got in touch with him, actually, in Israel.
And then, from Israel, we were in touch.
Well, I wrote a friend of mine, this Michael [NON-ENGLISH].
He has a sister that still lives there, in the city of Kostopol.
And she was the one that corresponded with him.
And this is how she told me that he lives in the city of Kostopol.
He got married, and he had at that time three daughters-- Claudia, Sophia, and Asha.
And they were in Kostopol.
And then, when I was in Montreal, I helped them.
I send them some money that they could buy a visa to go to Poland.
And then they also left to Poland from the Ukraine.
Yeah, it was true.
True and--
Well, when we were in hiding, my brother dug it all up and sold it to some of the neighbors for food.
Did you ever go back to Male Verbche after the partisans?
Yes.
When were were in Stepan, I used to go there.
And then I was in touch with that some of the people that helped us many years after.
But then when you weren't allowed to send any letters anymore-- I used to mail letters, but they never received them because the communist authorities have intercepted them.
And then I stopped.
What were the people like to you, when you went back to Male Verbche?
Oh, they were very nice to me, very, very nice.
They were very happy that we survived.
And now, they know about it because I did send some letters.
And, at one time, I even sent some parcels.
One of my friend's girlfriends, and she send me a monument-- picture of a monument in Rivne, which was built right after the war.
My father had a brother, Chaim.
And my mother-- and he had three sisters.
One sister that lived in the town of Sarny, and this is the son that was in, in Israel, I met-- Shabtai Waks.
So that sister.
And then, he had two other sisters, which I don't even remember their names.
They lived in other cities.
And my mother had three sisters.
And I don't know if she had a brother.
The one who was my aunt, I knew.
And I had the one or two others.
They were married later on somewhere in other cities.
No.
I don't remember.
Well, at Air Canada I had to work shifts, changing every week, a new shift.
And I got a little bit some stomach problems.
And the doctor didn't like that the shifts, and so on.
So he suggested maybe I should get another occupation.
And I met a gentleman that was a salesman for a company in home improvement, by the name of [?
Roscoe ?].
And he suggested I apply there.
And I applied, and I worked there for many, many years as a salesman.
And I never was a salesman.
But I didn't know that I could make it.
And I was a very good salesman in there.
Well, in 1982 my eldest daughter, Anna, she finished the first one, university as an occupational therapist.
And she came here to do her interning as an occupational therapist.
And she decided to stay in Toronto.
She didn't want to go back to Montreal.
Therefore, I decided that maybe I should try too to move to Toronto.

And the boss here at the company I worked for had to mentioned to me many times if I want to go to work for same company here, he would be glad to move-- pay for my move, and at the same time, guarantee me a salary.
So I came here for one year, tried it out, I liked it.
Everything worked out OK.
Everything worked out OK.
Then my other daughter, Yona, finished a bachelor's at McGill in commerce came to Toronto, too, and got a job-- a very good job with a bank.
Then my youngest one, Sheila, finished McGill as an engineer, mechanical engineer.
And she moved here.
And my wife was a teacher in Montreal, with a public Jewish school-- people's school called Judische Volkshochschule.
And she moved a year later when I was here already to Toronto.
And we bought this house.
We got all settled.
We're all happy.
Then, thank god, the kids, like I said-- the girls-- got all settled on their jobs.
Anna got married to a nice young man, Jerry Silverberg.
And they have now a boy, nine months old, called Jason Saul.
And a lovely young couple, and a beautiful grandson, I have.
And then, Yona got married to Earl Weiner, also a young man, a nice man.
And they have a son named Colby after my brother-- alav hashalom-- Yakov.
And he's is three and a half years old.
And then Sheila got married to a nice young man, Frank Friedman.
And they have a beautiful daughter named Eliza, four years old.
All my son-in-laws are graduated of university.
Frank is a CA.
In the beginning, I didn't want to talk to anyone about the experience.
I just couldn't face it.
But later on, when I was older, I started to tell them a little bit-- my wife, and everybody else-- a little bit about it.
But I didn't want to talk.
But now, in the last 10, 15 years, I've started to talk about it.
And they are all aware of all what I went through.

Well, first I would like the people that someday when we are gone, the-- want survivors of the Holocaust to see what happened to us and how it happened and how innocent people were taken and being tortured and murdered.
And the world should know what happened to us.
And I hope it will never happen again, that people will learn from this that to be on guard for, for Nazis like Hitler, and all the other Nazis of the-- of the world that are still around today.
[SIGHS] I don't know.
Sometimes I do, and sometimes I don't.
It's hard say.
I hope I can believe.
Oh yes, to me it's very important that the grandchildren to remain Jewish.
Our children all went to-- all of them-- my daughters and their husbands-- went to Jewish schools.
And they know all about Jewishness-- Yiddishkeit.
We, we keep a kosher home, and our kids do.
And their children, the grandchildren, God willing, now, the ones that are starting, are starting to learn in a Jewish school.
My wife, herself, is a teacher at USDS there.
And we do want to promote Yiddishkeit and should be a continuation.
And we should never know of what happened to the Jewish people.
you.
